question: A 16-year-old man was hospitalized after a snake bite and received blood transfusion and antivenom during his stay. Several days later he developed sudden right-sided weakness, cerebellar ataxia, and aphasia, prompting urgent neuroimaging. Which treatment-related or bite-related complication best explains this new clinical course and the imaging findings?

options.D: Septic embolic ischemic stroke from wound infection

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image for objective findings. The axial noncontrast head CT slice shows a well-defined hyperdense focus in the left cerebellar hemisphere marked by the arrow, consistent with acute parenchymal hemorrhage. I do not see signs of an extra-axial collection on this slice.

Next, I relate what I see to the clinical question. The patient developed sudden focal neurological deficits (right-sided weakness, cerebellar ataxia, and aphasia) several days after a snake bite and receiving transfusion/antivenom. The imaging demonstrates acute intracranial bleeding, which can explain the new, sudden focal neurologic signs (the cerebellar hemorrhage accounting for ataxia and a contralateral focal lesion explaining focal weakness/aphasia if additional hemorrhages are present).

Then I consider how each option aligns with those observations. Option A explicitly names venom-induced coagulopathy causing intracranial hemorrhage — this directly matches the CT appearance of acute parenchymal blood and the sudden focal deficits. Option B (antivenom-related anaphylaxis) would produce systemic allergic manifestations rather than a focal intracranial hyperdense hemorrhage, so it does not account for the CT finding. Option C (TRALI) refers to acute lung injury after transfusion and would not explain an isolated intracranial hemorrhage on CT. Option D (septic embolic ischemic stroke) implies ischemic infarction from infection-related emboli, which does not fit the acute hyperdense parenchymal bleed seen on this scan.

In summary, the image shows acute intracerebral hemorrhage that best fits the option describing venom-induced coagulopathy causing intracranial hemorrhage.
caption: Cerebral CT showing spontaneous left cerebellar hemorrhage, in a 16-year-old male presenting with loss of consciousness and severe anemia following snake bite
sub_caption: Cerebral CT showing spontaneous left cerebellar hemorrhage, in a 16-year-old male presenting with loss of consciousness and severe anemia following snake bite.
image_context:
A 16-year-old man, residing in a rural area 135 km from Ouagadougou, without past medical history, was referred to the Emergency Department for a snake bite of undetermined nature on December 12, 2018. The bite was located on the left arm which was completely distorted and painful. At the bite, there was a puntiform and ulcerative bleeding wound. The patient presented to a local medical center with significant bleeding at the insertion site of the venous tract, followed by a loss of consciousness. He had severe anemia (Hemoglobin at 5 g / dl) and received a blood transfusion of 2 blood bags. Following admission in the Infectious disease department, the patient endorsed acute headache without vomiting and constipation. Consciousness was unclear with obnibulation. There the patient had elevated blood pressure at 140/100 mmHg, normal body temperature, normal heart beat and a polypnea of 16 cycles per mm. Local examination revealed swelling of the left side of the upper limb and a lesion suggestive of the bite site. This envenomation was classified as grade 3 according to the Snakebite Severity Score. Biologically, white blood cells were at 7060 / mm3, C reactive protein at 8.39 mg / l, hemoglobin at 9.9 g / dl, VGM at 8.2, MCHC at 30.3, platelet count at 510000 / mm 3. Electrolyte test revealed a serum albumin level at 142.5 mmol / l, potassium at 4.24, calcium at 21.16. HSV serology was negative. After a few days following admission, the patient presented with sudden right hemiparesis (MRC: 0/5 at upper limb and 5/5 at lower limb), cerebellar ataxia and aphasia and then transferred later in the neurology department. The cerebral CT showed bilateral spontaneous frontal and left cerebellar hemorrhage (Fig. <xref rid="40794_2021_150_Fig2_HTML" ref-type="fig">2</xref>). The patient was treated with ceftriaxone and received a dose of antivenom serum during this hospitalization of 23 days. The clinical outcome was favorable and the patient recovered completely from this motor deficit.
Fig. 2Cerebral CT showing spontaneous left cerebellar hemorrhage, in a 16-year-old male presenting with loss of consciousness and severe anemia following snake bite
